role_summary: San Diego CORE founder and the city's principal civil rights leader of the 1960s; first African American to hold administrative rank at San Diego State University (1959); namesake of SDSU's Civil Rights and African American Experience Collection (2011).
ai_reading: Brown's top semantic neighbors cross ethnic and institutional categories more than is typical in this catalog: NAACP LDF lawyer Robert L. Carter (entry 108, count 181), Chicano civil rights leader Mateo Camarillo (entry 86, count 178), athlete-activist Bill Russell (entry 8, count 110), Robert Brown (entry 106), and West-Coast Black author Mildred Pitts Walter (entry 91). The embedding therefore reads his San Diego CORE leadership as a cross-coalition voice the cultural record's local-Black-organizer label does not foreground; the West Coast / cross-racial / cross-discipline pattern is unusual in a corpus where most clusters concentrate within a single faction or geography. His Tactical Pragmatism vs Strategic Vision position is -0.51, well toward Tactical Pragmatism, consistent with the CORE-chapter-founder and frequent-arrest framing his oral history record carries.
biographical_paragraph: Harold K. Brown was San Diego's principal civil rights leader of the 1960s and a CORE founder in the city; his 1959 graduation from San Diego State University placed him as the first African American to hold administrative rank on that campus [src: 2]. His arrest record across the city's segregation protests was extensive [src: 2]. In 2011 San Diego State established the Harold K. Brown Civil Rights and African American Experience Collection, archiving thousands of photographs, documents, and oral accounts from the civil rights movement under his name [src: 3]. He was born May 6, 1934 and died on his ninety-second birthday, May 6, 2026 [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 55 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].
movement_context: The Congress of Racial Equality (CORE) that Brown organized for in San Diego was founded in 1942 in Chicago by James Farmer, George Houser, Bernice Fisher, and a small Fellowship of Reconciliation cohort committed to applying Gandhian nonviolent direct action to U.S. racial segregation; the chapter-federated structure CORE adopted from its founding meant that a city-level founder like Brown was both the chapter's institutional anchor and the link between local protest tactics and the national CORE leadership Farmer ran from New York [src: 4, 5]. The 1960-1964 period in which Brown's San Diego chapter formed was the documented peak of CORE chapter expansion across the country: August Meier and Elliott Rudwick's institutional history records that CORE's chapter count and direct-action activity rose sharply after the Greensboro sit-ins of February 1960 and the Farmer-organized 1961 Freedom Rides put CORE on the national civil rights stage alongside SNCC and SCLC [src: 4]. West Coast CORE chapters in this period concentrated their organizing on three issues distinct from the southern movement's voter-registration focus: employment discrimination at major retailers and contractors, fair-housing campaigns against the racially restrictive covenants and lending practices that segregated California neighborhoods, and police-misconduct accountability; Mark Brilliant's institutional history of California civil rights organizing documents the specific organizing arenas West Coast CORE chapters worked in during this period and the cross-racial coalition character that distinguished California campaigns from southern ones [src: 6]. Brown's cross-coalition embedding signature with NAACP LDF, Chicano civil rights, and athlete-activist neighbors, surfaced in the AI's reading above, is structurally consistent with the cross-racial coalition pattern Brilliant documents for California's civil rights organizations in this period; San Diego's demographic composition, with substantial Black, Mexican American, and Asian American communities, made cross-racial coalition work both available and necessary for the CORE chapter's effectiveness [src: 6]. The Brown Collection at SDSU is the institutional record of the campaigns Brown's chapter ran in San Diego across this period [src: 3].
legacy_and_reception: The institutional record of Brown's legacy concentrates at San Diego State University, where he earned his degree in 1959 and where the Harold K. Brown Civil Rights and African American Experience Collection was established in 2011 to house photographs, documents, and oral accounts from the San Diego civil rights movement under his name [src: 3]. The Library of Congress's inclusion of his interview as entry 55 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) places his testimony in the same federally curated oral-history record that documents the principal organizers of the movement nationally [src: 1]; the Civil Rights History Project was created by 2009 federal legislation (Public Law 111-19, the Civil Rights History Project Act) directing the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian's National Museum of African American History and Culture to record and preserve oral histories of movement participants [src: 7]. Independent scholarly biographical treatment of Brown's San Diego organizing is sparse in the published institutional-history literature outside the SDSU collection's own finding aid and the LoC interview record; the catalog will expand this section as additional citable sources surface.

gallery.alt: James L. Farmer Jr. mugshot, Jackson, Mississippi police department, May 24, 1961
gallery.caption: James Farmer, CORE national director and co-founder, in his Jackson, Mississippi police mugshot after his arrest as a Freedom Rider on May 24, 1961. CORE's national chapter structure that Farmer led from New York was the network within which Brown organized San Diego CORE during the 1960-1964 chapter-expansion period.

gallery.alt: Attorney General Robert F. Kennedy addressing a CORE rally outside the Justice Department, June 14, 1963
gallery.caption: Attorney General Robert F. Kennedy speaking to a Congress of Racial Equality rally outside the U.S. Department of Justice on June 14, 1963. The photograph documents the period when CORE's national prominence, built through Farmer's leadership and the 1961 Freedom Rides, drew federal-executive attention; Brown's San Diego chapter was organizing during the same window.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (U.S. News and World Report collection, Library of Congress)

gallery.alt: Hepner Hall, San Diego State University, June 2013
gallery.caption: Hepner Hall, the historic Mission Revival administration building on the San Diego State University campus. SDSU is the institution from which Brown graduated in 1959, the first African American to hold administrative rank on the campus, and where the Harold K. Brown Civil Rights and African American Experience Collection was established in 2011.

sources.publisher: Oxford University Press, 1973; reissued University of Illinois Press, 1975; the standard institutional history of CORE's founding, chapter federation, and 1960s direct-action peak

sources.title: James Farmer, Lay Bare the Heart: An Autobiography of the Civil Rights Movement
sources.publisher: Arbor House, 1985; CORE national director's memoir of the chapter-federated organizing structure within which local CORE founders like Brown operated

sources.title: Mark Brilliant, The Color of America Has Changed: How Racial Diversity Shaped Civil Rights Reform in California, 1941-1978
sources.publisher: Oxford University Press, 2010; institutional history of California's distinctive cross-racial civil rights organizing arena

sources.title: Civil Rights History Project Act of 2009 (Public Law 111-19)
